pages.text: the details of the speciﬁc application [19]. The speciﬁc silane was selected due to its proven compatibility with epoxy adhesives [9]. The silane was mixed and applied according to the recommen- dations of the manufacturer which included diluting the silane in methanol to a 4% mixture by weight, brushing the mixture onto the steel surface and allowing it to dry for approximately 1 h. The CFRP strips were prefabricated with a disposable peel-ply to protect the surface. The strips were cut to the required dimen- sions and the edges were wiped with methanol to remove any residual dust. The peel-ply was removed immediately prior to bonding. The adhesive was mixed thoroughly by hand and applied to the CFRP surface. For the Detail AG and AGS specimens one layer of unidirectional glass ﬁbers was cut to the same dimensions as the CFRP strip, pressed into the adhesive and thoroughly impregnated by pressing with a plastic spatula. Additional adhesive was applied as needed to ensure that the ﬁbers were thoroughly impregnated. The CFRP plate was pressed onto the surface and clamped into place. The excess adhesive was removed and the specimens were left to cure in the ﬁxture at room temperature for at least 12 h. Measurement of specimens fabricated using a similar process indi- cated that this fabrication process resulted in an average cured adhesive thickness of 0.1 mm for specimens prepared without glass and 0.5 mm for specimens which included a glass ﬁber layer. 3.1. Environmental conditioning The overall testing program for the environmental durability study is given in Table 1 . The program includes 14 control speci- mens that were tested under laboratory conditions without any environmental conditioning. A total of 30 specimens were exposed to severe exposure conditions to accelerate the deterioration of the bond. This allowed comparison of the test results after a short duration of exposure. The accelerated exposure consisted of 1 week wet/1 week dry cycles in a 5% NaCl solution at a tempera- ture of 38 /C176C. During the wet portion of the cycle, the specimens were totally submerged in the NaCl solution and the temperature was maintained by a submersible water heater. During the dry cy- cle the specimens were exposed to ambient out-door tempera- tures. The specimens were protected from exposure to direct sunlight and rain. The experimental program included exposure durations of 1, 4 and 6 months. The accelerated exposure testing program was conducted to evaluate the relative performance of different methods of bonding the CFRP materials to the steel sur- face within a reasonable time frame. The accelerated testing may not accurately reﬂect the rate of degradation that can be expected under the effect of natural exposure conditions. The effect of sustained load on the environmental durability of the system was also considered. A total of 24 out of the 30 speci- mens subjected to severe exposure conditions were also subjected to a sustained load level of 15 kN. The applied load level represents 35% of the average ultimate strength of the control specimens which were bonded with adhesive only, T A. The specimens were conditioned in specially designed reinforced concrete tanks as shown in Fig. 3a. The tanks also acted as self-reacting frames to re- sist the applied load which was applied to the specimens. The sus- tained load was applied using a specially designed ﬁxture, shown in Fig. 3 b, and the load level was monitored using two electrical r

after removal from the conditioning tank indicated noticeable cor- rosion of the steel. Deposition of the corrosion products caused dis- coloration of the surface of the FRP. There was no noticeable difference in the degree of degradation between the different bond conﬁgurations. Visual inspection did not reveal any debonded areas for any of the specimens. During the residual strength tests, all of the specimens failed due to debonding of the CFRP strips from the steel surface in a similar manner to the control specimens. Fail- ure of the Detail AGS specimens occurred within the glass ﬁber layer with some of the glass ﬁbers remaining bonded to the CFRP strip and some remaining bonded to the steel surface. Dry glass ﬁ- bers were observed along the failure surface indicating incomplete wetting of the ﬁbers due to the relatively high viscosity of the adhesive. 4.2. Test results after 4 months exposure A total of ten specimens were subjected to environmental con- ditioning for 4 months. After 4 months of environmental condi- tioning, one of the CFRP strips was totally debonded from the steel surface of one specimens that was bonded with adhesive only and subjected to a sustained load. The environmental exposure apparently caused degradation of the bond which led to failure of the specimen under the effect the sustained load which was equivalent to 35% of the joint capacity. Inspection of the remaining specimens indicated partial debonding of one of the CFRP strips for one of the unloaded specimens which was bonded with adhesive only and exposed to environmental conditions. The remaining specimens exhibited noticeable degradation including severe cor- rosion of the steel and build-up of corrosion products on and dis- coloration of the CFRP. No debonding was observed for any of the other specimens. During the residual strength tests all of the tested specimens failed by debonding of the CFRP plates. The failure surface of the remaining ‘A’ specimen revealed the presence of a discolored, par- tially debonded region near the edges of the CFRP strip as shown in Fig. 5b. This suggests possible ingress of moisture into the interfa- cial region between the adhesive and the steel. The failure surface of one of the ‘AS’ specimens, which was pretreated with a silane coupling agent, indicates a complete bond between the steel and the CFRP strip without any discoloration or indication of degrada- tion of the bond interface as shown in Fig. 5 b. This suggests that the presence of the silane prevented ingress of moisture into the interfacial region and helped to enhance the environmental dura- bility of the bond. The failure surface for the ‘AGS’ specimens, which included both silane and a glass ﬁber layer, revealed that the glass ﬁber layer within the adhesive was largely unimpregnat- ed with adhesive as shown in Fig. 5 c. The dry glass ﬁbers had a rust-colored discoloration which suggests wicking of moisture and deposition of corrosion products, which were suspended in the solution, along the dry ﬁbers. 4.3. Test results after 6 months exposure The remaining ten double-lap shear specimens were exposed to severe environmental conditions for 6 months. Eight of the speci- mens were also subjected to a sustained load while the two remaining ‘A’ specimens, which were bonded by a thin layer of adhesive only, were left unloaded. It should be noted that one of the loaded ‘A’ specimens failed by debonding of one of the CFRP strips after only 17 weeks of exposure

the remaining loaded ‘A’ specimen and one of the unloaded speci- mens both exhibited partial debonding of one of the CFRP strips. The residual strength tests indicated that the failure modes of the remaining specimens were similar to those of the 4 month specimens. 5. Bond strength degradation The measured ultimate capacities of all of the tested specimens are given in Table 2 . Comparison of the average ultimate strength for the ‘A’ and ‘AS’ control specimens suggests that the use of the silane adhesion promoter increased the average measured bond strength by 20%. However, some of the observed difference may be due to the statistical variation of the bond strength rather than due to the effect of the silane adhesion promoter. Comparison of the ‘A’ and ‘AG’ control specimens indicates that the presence of the glass ﬁber layer increased the average mea- sured bond strength by 74%. This suggests that the glass ﬁbers acted as reinforcement for the adhesive and retarded the propaga- tion of cracks through the adhesive layer. However, the measured strength of the ‘AG’ and ‘AGS’ control specimens also exhibited a signiﬁcantly higher coefﬁcient of variation than the specimens without glass ﬁbers in the adhesive layer. The increase of the var- iability of the bond strength may have possibly been due to the ob- served shifting of the glass ﬁbers within the adhesive layer during fabrication which was previously noted. The measured degrada- tion of the tension strength of the tested double-lap shear speci- mens, which is given in Table 2 , is presented graphically in Fig. 6 for the four different bond details for comparison purposes. The range of the measured results is represented by the error bars in the ﬁgure. Comparison of the test results shown in Fig. 6 for the Detail A specimens indicates a 60% reduction of the measured tension strength after 6 months of environmental conditioning. This high- lights the need to include some form of supplemental protection to enhance the environmental durability of the system. It is interest- ing to note that the measured strength of the ‘A’ specimens in- creased slightly after 1 month of conditioning. The measured increase of strength may represent the inherent statistical varia- tion of the strength of the bond. It may also possibly be due to post curing of the adhesive due to the elevated temperature associated with the environmental conditioning. Comparison of the results for the ‘AS’ specimens in Fig. 6 indi- cates that the presence of the silane adhesion promoter signiﬁ- cantly enhanced the environmental durability of the bond. No signiﬁcant degradation of the bond strength was measured after completion of the 6 month duration of the environmental condi- tioning. The enhancement of the durability could be due to the for- mation of primary chemical bonds at the interface between the steel and the adhesive due to the presence of the silane. Silanes are monomeric silicone based chemicals similar to hydrocarbons [20]. These chemicals typically include similar hydrocarbon groups, called organic functional groups, to those found in organic, carbon based, compounds such as epoxy adhesives. The organic functional groups can react chemically in the same manner on the silane molecule as on an organic molecule. Therefore a primary chemical bond can form between the organic functional group on the silane molecule and the adhesive. The other end of the silane molecule consists of a silicone based inorganic function

Environmental durability of a CFRP system for strengthening steel structures Mina Dawood a,*, Sami Rizkalla b a University of Houston, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, N107 Engineering Building 1, 4800 Calhoun St., Houston, TX 77204-4003, USA b North Carolina State University, Constructed Facilities Laboratory, Campus Box 7533, 2414 Campus Shore Dr., Raleigh, NC 27695-7533, USA article info Article history: Received 17 November 2008 Received in revised form 3 April 2009 Accepted 9 February 2010 Available online 17 March 2010 Keywords: Fiber reinforced polymers Durability Bonding Adhesives Steel Silane Corrosion abstract Recently a high modulus CFRP system was developed to enhance the load carrying capacity and service- ability of steel bridges and structures. However, the environmental durability of the system has not yet been demonstrated. This paper presents the ﬁndings of a research program that was conducted to eval- uate the environmental durability of the bond of the proposed CFRP strengthening system to steel sur- faces. The program consisted of testing 44 steel-CFRP double-lap shear specimens. The specimens were exposed to severe environmental conditions for different durations, up to 6 months. Different methods to enhance the bond durability were studied including pre-treating the steel surface with a silane coupling agent, inserting a glass ﬁber layer within the adhesive and a combination of both methods of protection. The research ﬁndings indicate that the use of a silane coupling agent signiﬁcantly enhanced the bond durability. While the presence of the glass ﬁbers helped to enhance the initial bond strength of the sys- tem, it did not improve the durability of the bond. The use of both techniques enhanced both the overall bond strength and the environmental durability of the strengthening system. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Research on the use of CFRP materials for strengthening steel structures has been ongoing for more than 10 years. A number of studies have been conducted to evaluate the ﬂexural behavior of beams strengthened with CFRP, buckling behavior of slender and tubular members subjected to compression loading, use of CFRP materials for repair of cracked steel members and bond behavior of CFRP materials to steel surfaces [1]. However, comparably little is known about the environmental durability of the bond between CFRP materials and steel surfaces. While it is well known that exposure to environmental conditions can reduce the strength of bonded joints, other researchers have demonstrated that environ- mental exposure can also reduce the ductility of bonded joints be- tween CFRP materials and steel surfaces [2]. In most bonding applications, the ingress of water can cause se- vere deterioration of the bonded joint. Water can penetrate a bonded joint by diffusion through the adhesive, wicking along the interfaces between the adhesive and the adherends, capillary action through cracks and voids in the adhesive or absorption through porous adherends [3]. Once moisture has penetrated into the joint, degradation can occur due to one of two primary mech- anisms: degradation of the adhesive/adherend interface or degra- dation of the properties of the adhesive itself [3]. At the interface, degradation can occur due to displacement of intermo- lecular adhesive forces or due to instability of the oxide layers on the surface of metallic adherends. Further, the presence of m

1.1. Galvanic corrosion Galvanic corrosion can occur when two metals are coupled to- gether and submerged in an electrolyte. Four conditions are neces- sary for galvanic corrosion to occur [10]: (i) the two metals in contact must exhibit a sufﬁcient difference of electrical potential (ii) an electrical connection must exist between the two metals, (iii) the two metals must be bridged by an electrolyte and (iv) a sustained cathodic reaction must proceed on the more electropos- itive metal which typically involves the consumption of dissolved oxygen. If these four conditions are satisﬁed, a galvanic current will form between the two metals. The current will increase the corro- sion rate of the anode and may decrease or halt the corrosion of the cathode. To help minimize the occurrence of galvanic corrosion be- tween steel and CFRP materials, an electrical insulator can be placed between the two materials to break the electrical contact and prevent the formation of a galvanic current. Tavakkolizadeh and Saadatmnesh studied the effect of using an epoxy adhesive as an insulator between steel and carbon ﬁbers [11]. They found that coating the carbon ﬁbers with a 0.25 mm thick layer of adhe- sive reduced the galvanic corrosion rate of steel in seawater by 21 times compared to uncoated ﬁbers. Another study suggested that embedding a layer of glass ﬁbers in the adhesive between the steel and the CFRP helped to prevent electrical contact between the two materials, particularly in locations where there may be a void in the adhesive [9]. 2. Material properties of the strengthening system The proposed CFRP strengthening system consists of pultruded high modulus CFRP plates which are bonded to the tension surface of the steel member by an epoxy adhesive. The average measured tensile modulus and ultimate strength of the CFRP used in the pro- posed strengthening system were 418,000 MPa and 1490 MPa respectively [12]. The CFRP strips were bonded to the steel surface using a two-part epoxy adhesive which was cured under ambient room temperature conditions. The details of the adhesive selection process for the proposed strengthening system are presented by Schnerch [13]. The average measured tensile modulus and ultimate strength of the cured adhesive were 2980 MPa and 38 MPa respec- tively [14]. Both the CFRP and adhesive materials exhibited essen- tially linear-elastic behavior to failure. The glass transition temperature of the adhesive was determined using Differential Scanning Calorimetry according to ASTM E 1356-03 [15,16]. The midpoint glass transition temperature of the cured adhesive, T m, was found to be 62 /C176C. The steel materials used in the experimental program had an average elastic modulus and 0.2% offset yield strength of 215,000 MPa and 334 MPa respectively [17]. 3. Experimental program The experimental program consisted of 44 steel-CFRP double- lap shear specimens that were tested to evaluate the environmen- tal durability of the proposed CFRP strengthening system. Typical test specimens consisted of two 9.5 mm thick /C2 32 mm wide steel plates bonded together using two 4 mm thick /C2 19 mm wide CFRP strips as shown in Fig. 1. The width of the CFRP strips was selected to be less than the width of the steel plates to represent a typical ﬁeld strengthening application in which the entire width of the tension ﬂange may not be covered with CFRP, therefore leaving a portion of the ﬂange exposed to environmental action. The exper- imental pr

Therefore, the reduction factor proposed in the Italian guideline is appropriate for bonded joints prepared using a suitable adhesion promoter. However, for bonded joints which do not include silane the reduction factor may be somewhat unconservative. However, this is based on the ﬁndings of a limited number of accelerated exposure environmental tests which do not represent the actual service environment of most typical structures. The British and Italian guidelines include reduction factors of 2.0 (divisor) and 0.8 (multiplier) respectively to account for the ef- fect of sustained load. The ﬁndings of the current study indicate that the presence of the applied sustained load did not have a sig- niﬁcant effect of the degradation of the bond strength of the tested specimens. The magnitude of the applied load in the current study was approximately 35% of the measured initial strength of the bonded joints which was applied for a maximum duration of 6 months. Therefore, when the magnitude of the applied load is relatively low the reduction factor of the Italian guidelines may be used. However, for higher load levels and longer durations, the reduction factor of the British guideline may be more appropriate. 9. Conclusions This paper presents the ﬁndings of an experimental program that was conducted to evaluate the environmental durability of the bond between CFRP materials and steel surfaces. Accelerated environmental exposure tests were conducted, under conditions that were far more severe than natural environmental conditions, to evaluate the relative performance of four different bonding tech- niques. Specimens that were bonded using a thin layer of adhesive exhibited a 60% degradation of the measured bond strength after 6 months of exposure to severe environmental conditions with some specimens debonding spontaneously during the exposure period. Specimens that were pretreated with a silane coupling agent prior to bonding exhibited essentially no degradation of the bond strength over the 6 month exposure duration. Specimens that included a glass ﬁber insulating layer within the adhesive exhibited an increase of the average initial bond strength of the system as compared to specimens that were bonded without a glass ﬁber layer. However, the durability of these specimens was similar to that of specimens that were bonded with adhesive only. Specimens that included both a glass ﬁber layer and silane pre- treatment exhibited enhanced initial strength and durability. The ﬁndings suggest that the degradation of the bond strength was likely due primarily to the degradation of the interface be- tween the steel and the adhesive. The durability of the interface re- gion was enhanced due to the primary chemical bonds formed by the silane coupling agent as described previously. Degradation of the adhesive properties may have also contributed to the degrada- tion, but the role of this mechanism was apparently secondary. Based on the research ﬁndings, for ﬁeld applications in which the strengthening system will be exposed to a moist environment, pre- treatment of the steel surface with silane is highly recommended. Based on the ﬁndings of the current study, which included a limited number of tests using accelerated environmental condi- tions, both the British and the Italian guidelines can be used to pre- dict the expected strength degradation of steel-CFRP bonded joints exposed to environmental conditions. The selection of the appro- priate design pro

formed by the silane molecule are stronger, and therefore more environmentally stable, than the secondary van der Waals forces which are attributed to adhesive forces. A similar trend was observed for the ‘AG’ and ‘AGS’ specimens, as shown in Fig. 6. These bond details included an additional layer of glass ﬁber embedded in the adhesive layer. The glass ﬁbers helped to increase the initial bond strength of the tested double- lap shear specimens. However, the ‘AG’ specimens exhibited a 55% degradation of the average measured strength after 6 months of environmental exposure. This is comparable to the degradation of the ‘A’ specimens which were bonded with adhesive only. This suggests that the presence of the glass ﬁbers did not signiﬁcantly increase the durability of the system. By comparison, the ‘AGS’ specimens, which included both silane and glass ﬁbers, exhibited a signiﬁcant increase of the initial bond strength as compared to the ‘A’ specimens while exhibiting only a minimal degradation of the bond strength. It is interesting to note that the coefﬁcient of variation of the measured strength of the environmentally conditioned ‘AG’ and ‘AGS’ specimens was lower than that of the control specimens. This was mainly due to the difference of the number of repeated tests that were conducted in each case. For the ‘AG’ and ‘AGS’ bond de- tail, four control specimens were tested. However, only two spec- imens of each bond detail were exposed to the accelerated environmental conditions for each of the given exposure durations. Therefore the variation of the measured strength was higher for the control specimens due to the larger sample size. 6. Effect of sustained loading conditions The measured residual strength of the specimens that were bonded with a thin layer of adhesive only and exposed to the com- bined effect of environmental conditions and sustained load for durations of 1, 4 and 6 months were compared with the results of similar specimens which were exposed to environmental condi- tions only as shown in Fig. 7 . The ﬁgure indicates that the mea- sured average tension strength of the unloaded specimens was slightly higher than that of the loaded specimens after 4 and 6 months of conditioning. However, the variation is within the experimental scatter of the results. It is likely that the applied load level was not sufﬁcient to induce signiﬁcant cracking or distress to the bonded interface and therefore the applied load did not have a signiﬁcant effect on the bond durability. 7. Material degradation To evaluate the effect of the environmental conditioning on the material properties of the steel, CFRP and adhesive, tension speci- mens of the materials were exposed to the same environmental conditions as the double-lap shear specimens. The degradation of the material properties of the steel, CFRP and adhesive is presented in Fig. 8 a–c respectively. The degradation of the material proper- ties shown in Fig. 8 is related to the measured initial properties which are given above. Fig. 8a shows the degradation of the yield strength, stiffness and mass of the tested steel coupons for the different exposure dura- tions considered. The values are normalized with respect to the average parameter values for the unconditioned coupons. The experimental scatter of the results is shown by the error bars in the ﬁgure. The ﬁgure indicates that the degradation of the three parameters is comparable throughout the 6 month exposure dura- tion with a maximum

cial region between the steel and the adhesive. Wicking of mois- ture along the dry glass ﬁbers or absorption of moisture into the adhesive may also have affected the bond durability as indicated by the observed failure modes of the tested double-lap shear specimens. 8. Comparison to existing design guidelines Several guidelines have been published to provide recommen- dations regarding the design and installation of FRP strengthening systems for rehabilitation of metallic structures [21,22]. The design approaches typically involve reducing the strength of the bonded joint by applying material partial factors to account for such effects as degradation of the material properties due to exposure to envi- ronmental conditions, inherent material variability, effect of sus- tained or repeated loading and reliability of the prediction model. The partial factors for environmental durability and sus- tained loading from the British guideline [21] and the Italian guide- line [22] are given in Tables 3 and 4 respectively. The partial factors should be applied so as to reduce the calculated strength of the system. Therefore, factors greater than unity are divisors while fac- tors less than unity are multipliers. Inspection of Table 3 indicates that, according to the British guideline, for strengthening systems in which the material proper- ties are determined under laboratory conditions which are differ- ent from the expected service conditions an environmental factor of 2.0 should be used. This corresponds to a strength degradation of 50% due to the effect of environmental exposure. This is consis- tent with the measured degradation of the Detail A and AG speci- mens, of 60% and 55% respectively, which were bonded without using a silane adhesion promoter. However, for the Detail AS and AGS specimens the degradation of the average measured strength did not exceed 16%. Thus, the environmental factor of 2.0 is overly conservative in this case. The British guideline also includes a re- ﬁned estimate to predict the environmental factor when the oper- ating conditions and the glass transition temperature of the adhesive are known: cme ¼ ﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃﬃ ðTg /C0 Tref Þ ðTg /C0 TÞ s ð1Þ where Tg is the glass transition temperature of the adhesive, Tref is the reference temperature at which the material properties are measured, and T is the operating temperature. For the proposed strengthening system the material properties were measured at a reference temperature, T ref,o f2 0 /C176C. The measured glass transition temperature of the adhesive, Tg,i s6 2 /C176C and the operating temper- ature of the specimens during the environmental conditioning was T =3 8 /C176C. Under these conditions, the calculated environmental fac- tor, cme, is 1.32. This corresponds to a strength degradation of 25% which is consistent with the degradation of the average measured strength for the tested specimens which were prepared using the si- lane adhesion promoter. Therefore, the research ﬁndings, which are based on a limited number of accelerated exposure tests, indicate that the basic factor of 2.0 of the British guideline is appropriate for bonded joints prepared without silane while if silane is applied the more reﬁned estimate may be used. Inspection of Table 4 indicates that, using the Italian guideline, for carbon/epoxy systems exposed to aggressive environments an environmental factor of 0.85 should be used.
